kept.text:
Perfect example of why the SaaSpocalypse is delusional. 

Buy the blood.

Nobody is going to vibe code alternatives to this stuff and then *maintain it.*

No company or team on earth, in their right mind, wants to be responsible for their own personal Figma and Intuit and Adobe and Office and lead management while also working on their own products.

author.name: Daniel Jeffries
author.description: Author, futurist, and systems architect. Recursively self improving.

quoted_tweet.text:
Agentic coding is a huge boon for software developers that want to get far more done, great for IT people to build vastly more custom systems internally, great for domain experts that want to automate workflows or wire systems together, and absolutely fantastic for anyone curious to learn how to start coding. 

What it’s less great for is casually building complex software that you have to maintain on an ongoing basis and take on all the risk for. Upgrades, maintenance, keeping up to date with latest security issues, and so on, are taxes most knowledge workers aren’t familiar with or prepared for.

Net net: we’re going to get 100X more software and vastly more software developers in the future. But that’s different from *everyone* rolling their own.

kept.text:
Interesting...

DMT evidence doesn't just illustrate Bohm's rheomode — it may provide the first empirical window into what rheomodic perception actually looks and feels like.

Bohm's problem, stated plainly.

Bohm invented the rheomode because he believed ordinary language encodes a false ontology. When we say "the electron orbits the nucleus," language forces us to posit two things (electron, nucleus) and then add a relationship between them (orbiting). The things come first; the process comes second. Bohm thought this was exactly backwards. There are no things. There is only process — a flowing, undivided holomovement — and what we call things are relatively stable patterns within that flow. The electron is not a noun that orbits; there is a verb — something like electroning — and the apparently stable particle is what that verb produces when conditions allow a standing pattern.

The rheomode was Bohm's attempt to build a language that gets this right. Verb-roots are primary. What a verb-root produces — its "product" — is secondary, derived, and potentially temporary. You don't start with things and add movements. You start with movements and notice where they produce temporary stabilities.

QPT adopted this at the formal level. Operations are verb-roots, cells are verb-products. Invariants are what persists through movement, not things extracted from movement. This is clean and well-stated. But it remains formal — it describes what the theory says about the relationship between operation and product. It doesn't tell you what rheomodic perception would be like from the inside. What would it actually feel like to perceive verb-roots rather than noun-products?

The DMT evidence suggests an answer.

Normal perception is noun-first.

In ordinary waking consciousness, you perceive things. A table. A wall. A person. A room. These things have properties (color, shape, texture) and they have relationships to each other (the table is near the wall, the person is in the room). Crucially, the things come first — they are perceptually primary. The properties and relationships are perceived of the things. You see the table and then you notice its color. You don't perceive color-ing and then notice that it has temporarily stabilized into a table-shaped pattern.

This noun-first character of normal perception is not accidental. It's what the reference cascade produces. The reference cascade tells each perceptual level what to expect, and what it expects are things — stable objects with persistent identities. Level 7 (Category) classifies perceptions into types. Level 8 (Sequence) organizes types into routines. Level 9 (Program) executes routines. All of these levels operate on noun-like entities — discrete, bounded, persistent objects that can be classified, sequenced, and manipulated.

The entire middle section of the PCT hierarchy — the Signetics scale that we noticed was absent from the DMT taxonomy — is the noun-generating machinery. Events are nouns (a discrete occurrence). Relationships are nouns (a persistent connection between things). Categories are nouns (a class to which things belong). Sequences are nouns (an ordered collection of things). This machinery takes the raw perceptual flow and carves it into discrete, stable, nameable entities that can be tracked, predicted, and acted upon.

The reference cascade enforces noun-first perception by telling lower levels to organize their output into thing-shaped packages. "Find objects." "Track boundaries." "Maintain identities across time." These are the reference states that the Semiosphere sends down through the Signetics into the Substrate. The Substrate perceptual machinery complies — not because it naturally produces things, but because it's been instructed to.

DMT perception is verb-first.

What happens when those instructions are removed? The DMT evidence shows us.

At Level 2 (Symmetry Hotel), surfaces tessellate. The patterns are not on the surfaces the way a painting is on a wall — they are the surfaces. There is no noun (wall) that has a property (pattern). There is patterning — a verb — and the wall is what the verb is currently doing. The wallpaper symmetry groups are not decorations applied to a thing; they are the visible trace of an operation (Ω, compression) acting in real time. The perceiver is seeing the verb of symmetry-finding rather than the noun of a patterned surface.

At Level 3 (Magic Eye), depth crystallizes from pattern. The phase transition is telling: one moment there's a flat patterned surface (a noun), the next moment there's depthward movement (a verb). The depth doesn't belong to anything. It's not the depth of the surface. It's depth-ing — the operation Φ⁻¹ (concretization) caught in the act of producing spatial structure. The perceiver witnesses the verb of concretization before it has finished producing its noun-product.

At Level 4 (Crystal Worlds), textures saturate. Again, the textures don't belong to objects. There are no crystals that have textures. There is crystallizing — a verb of textural differentiation (Δ) operating at maximal intensity. The world-sheet saturation Emilsson describes is the experience of a differentiation operation running without any noun to anchor its products.

At Level 5 (Waiting Room), modalities synchronize. This is perhaps the clearest case of verb-first perception. In normal consciousness, you see a thing and hear a thing and touch a thing — three noun-perceptions coordinated by the fact that they're about the same object. In the Waiting Room, there is no thing being perceived across modalities. There is perceiving itself — a single verb that manifests visually, auditorially, and tactilely simultaneously. The cross-modal binding isn't "the same object detected by multiple senses." It's a single operation (◉, Holon) manifesting across channels. The perceiver experiences the unity of the perceiving-verb, not the identity of a perceived-noun.

At Level 6 (Breakthrough), the very space in which things could exist undergoes transformation. This is the deepest rheomodic perception: the manifold itself is experienced as a verb — as space-ing or topology-ing — rather than as a fixed container for things. Wormholes are not holes in a thing. They are the manifold-generating operation performing a topological act that the noun-framework of ordinary spatial perception can't accommodate.

This is what Bohm's rheomode looks like from the inside.

Bohm could describe the rheomode linguistically and formally, but he couldn't point to an experience of it. He could say "the electron is a verb, not a noun" but he couldn't show anyone what it felt like to perceive verb-first rather than noun-first. The DMT evidence may provide exactly this.

Each DMT level, read through Bohm, is the progressive exposure of the verb-layer underneath the noun-layer:

Level 1 reveals that intensity is a verb (intensifying, not "an intensity"). Level 2 reveals that pattern is a verb (symmetrizing, not "a pattern"). Level 3 reveals that depth is a verb (concretizing, not "a depth"). Level 4 reveals that texture is a verb (differentiating, not "a texture"). Level 5 reveals that modality is a verb (perceiving, not "a perception"). Level 6 reveals that space is a verb (manifolding, not "a space"). And the dissolution of identity at Level 9 reveals that the self is a verb (selfing, not "a self").

In each case, what normal perception presents as a stable noun is revealed to be the temporarily-stable product of an ongoing operation. The operation was always running — it's what produces the noun in ordinary consciousness. But under normal conditions, the reference cascade hides the operation and presents only its product. DMT strips away the product-presentation and exposes the operation itself.

QPT's six operations as the six verbs of perception.

This reframes QPT's operations not as formal movements through an abstract matrix but as the actual verbs that the perceptual system performs to generate experience:

Φ (Unfold) is the verb of making explicit — the act by which the implicate becomes explicate. In DMT terms, it's what you see happening when patterns emerge from undifferentiated visual noise. The unfolding is not a metaphor; it's a perceptual act you can witness when the reference cascade stops hiding it.

Φ⁻¹ (Concretize) is the verb of making real — the act by which structure becomes experienced spatial fact. The Magic Eye crystallization is this verb caught in the act. Normally you never see concretization happening because it happens too fast and the result (solid-looking objects) is presented as given. DMT slows it down or makes it visible.

Ω (Compress) is the verb of finding invariance — the act by which the perceptual system discovers what remains the same across variation. The wallpaper symmetry groups are Ω's products, but under DMT you can see Ω operating: the tessellation is the compression verb actively seeking the highest-order symmetry it can impose. You're not seeing a pattern; you're seeing pattern-finding.

Δ (Differentiate) is the verb of distinguishing — the act by which unity becomes multiplicity. The Crystal Worlds' textural saturation is Δ running at maximum: every point in the visual field is being maximally distinguished from its neighbors. Under normal conditions, Δ is constrained to produce useful distinctions (object boundaries, figure-ground separation). Unconstrained, it reveals itself as an engine of maximal differentiation — every possible distinction gets made.

Ψ (Transfer) is the verb of crossing boundaries — the act by which content moves from one quadrant to another. Cross-modal binding is Ψ operating across sensory channels: content transfers from visual to tactile to auditory processing. Normally each channel's boundaries are enforced. DMT loosens the boundaries and lets Ψ operate freely, which is why modalities synchronize — the transfer verb runs across all channels without restriction.

◉ (Holon) is the verb of perceiving wholeness through any part — and this may be the verb that ties the entire DMT experience together. The sense that the experience is unified despite being multifaceted, that any single aspect of it gives access to the whole, that the depth and the texture and the pattern and the sound are all "the same thing perceived differently" — this is ◉ operating pervasively. In ordinary consciousness, ◉ is rare and high-level (a strategic insight, a moment of understanding the whole situation through one detail). Under DMT, ◉ operates at every level, which is why the experience has that characteristic quality of profound interconnectedness.

The implicate order as the perceptual ground state.

Here's where the DMT evidence and Bohm's philosophy converge most precisely. Bohm's implicate order is the enfolded whole from which the explicate order unfolds. QPT maps this to π — the absential ground, the not-yet-actual that shapes what can unfold.

We noted earlier that the DMT perceptual system has a natural resting state — what it gravitates toward when all constraints are removed: maximal symmetry, full cross-modal integration, open topology. We also noted that this resting state might correspond to π becoming experienceable rather than remaining purely absential.

Under the Bohmian reading, the natural resting state of the perceptual system is the implicate order — or rather, it's the closest the explicate perceptual system can come to perceiving the implicate order directly. Normally, Φ (unfolding) runs continuously, producing the explicate order of objects-in-space that we call ordinary consciousness. The reference cascade ensures that unfolding produces specific, stable, thing-shaped products. DMT disrupts the cascade, and without instructions about what products to produce, Φ partially reverses — or rather, Φ continues to operate but without the constraints that normally force its products into thing-shaped packages. The result is something between fully implicate (no distinctions at all) and fully explicate (stable objects in stable space). It's a partially-unfolded state where the operations themselves are visible but their products haven't yet stabilized into nouns.

This is precisely what Bohm's holomovement would look like if you could perceive it: not a static structure but an active process of unfolding and enfolding, with temporary stabilities forming and dissolving, with the whole present in every part (◉ operating pervasively), with symmetry as the natural tendency of the unconstrained flow.

The verb-preservation distinction becomes experiential.

QPT distinguishes between verb-preserving invariance (the operation persists but products are regenerated each time) and product-preserving invariance (the product persists across iterations). Under normal consciousness, most perception is product-preserving: you see the same table across time, the same room, the same body. Objects persist. The perceptual system's job is to maintain persistent products.

Under DMT, perception shifts toward verb-preserving. Nothing persists as a product — the tessellation changes, the depth reconfigures, the entities morph, the topology shifts. What persists is the verb — the patterning continues even as patterns change, the concretizing continues even as what's concrete shifts, the differentiating continues even as what's differentiated is replaced. The experience has continuity not because the same things keep existing but because the same operations keep operating.

This is what verb-preserving invariance feels like from the inside: a sense of continuity and coherence despite constant change. The identity of the experience is not in its content (which is in constant flux) but in its operations (which are consistent). You can't point to what persists because nothing noun-like persists. But something clearly does persist — the quality of the flow, the character of the movement, the way things keep changing even as what changes is never the same twice.

DMT users often report this as deeply paradoxical: the experience is simultaneously completely novel at every moment and completely familiar throughout. The novelty is in the products (never seen before). The familiarity is in the verbs (always operating). What the experiencer is recognizing as familiar is the perceptual system's own operations — the verb-roots that normally run invisibly behind the stable noun-products of ordinary consciousness.

The deepest implication: ordinary consciousness is a special case.

Bohm argued that the explicate order — the world of distinct objects in space and time — is not fundamental. It's a special case derived from a more fundamental implicate order by the operation of unfolding under specific constraints. Most physicists found this metaphysically interesting but practically inert, because the implicate order is not observable. You can't do experiments on it. You can only do experiments on the explicate products of unfolding.

The DMT evidence, if taken seriously, suggests that the implicate order — or something structurally isomorphic to it — is observable, under specific conditions. Not through physical instruments but through direct perceptual experience, when the constraints that normally produce the explicate order are pharmacologically disrupted. The natural resting state of the perceptual system, revealed when the reference cascade is disabled, is a state in which operations are visible, products are unstable, the whole is perceivable through any part, and the distinction between observer and observed becomes uncertain.

This doesn't prove Bohm's metaphysics. The perceptual system could be generating an internally-consistent hallucination of the implicate order without the implicate order actually existing as a feature of physics. But it does something Bohm could never do himself: it provides a phenomenological anchor for his concepts. When Bohm says "the implicate order," we can now point to a specific, reproducible, pharmacologically-accessible perceptual state and say "something like that — the experience of verb-roots without stable noun-products, of wholeness prior to division, of operations visible before their results solidify."

And it reframes ordinary consciousness in a way that aligns with both Bohm and QPT's processism: ordinary consciousness is not the baseline state of the perceptual system. It is what the perceptual system produces when the reference cascade constrains its natural verb-first, symmetry-seeking, holistic operation into a noun-first, error-minimizing, object-producing mode. It is the explicate order — a specific, functional, ecologically-necessary special case of a more general perceptual capacity that, left unconstrained, operates rheomodically.

QPT's formal claim that "operations are verb-roots; cells are verb-products" turns out to have an experiential correlate: there is a mode of perception in which you can actually experience the verb-roots operating prior to their products stabilizing. That mode is not ordinary consciousness. It's what ordinary consciousness is built from and continuously prevents you from seeing.
